verses.text: Now it came to pass after this, that Nahash the king of the children of Ammon died, and his son reigned in his stead. And David said, I will shew kindness unto Hanun the son of Nahash, because his father shewed kindness to me. And David sent messengers to comfort him concerning his father. So the servants of David came into the land of the children of Ammon to Hanun, to comfort him. But the princes of the children of Ammon said to Hanun, Thinkest thou that David doth honour thy father, that he hath sent comforters unto thee? are not his servants come unto thee for to search, and to overthrow, and to spy out the land? Wherefore Hanun took David's servants, and shaved them, and cut off their garments in the midst hard by their buttocks, and sent them away. Then there went certain, and told David how the men were served. And he sent to meet them: for the men were greatly ashamed. And the king said, Tarry at Jericho until your beards be grown, and then return. Guidance: David, remembering a past kindness from old king Nahash, seeks to honour the memory by sending comforters to his son Hanun in his grief. It is a generous, gracious act. But Hanun's suspicious princes poison his mind, persuading him that the ambassadors are spies, and Hanun cruelly humiliates them — shaving them and cutting off their garments — and sends them away in shame. The chapter is showing us how a malicious, suspicious heart turns kindness into supposed treachery; the princes could not believe in genuine goodwill, and so they read evil into David's mercy and provoked a needless war. The chapter is teaching us the great evil of a suspicious spirit that misjudges the motives of others — it not only wrongs the kind but breeds strife and ruin. Hanun, listening to slanderous counsel rather than receiving comfort, repaid good with insult and brought disaster on his people. The chapter is teaching us to beware of evil counselors and of the readiness to think the worst of others; many a friendship and many a peace have been destroyed by ungrounded suspicion. But the chapter also shows us the tenderness of David toward his disgraced servants: hearing how they were 'greatly ashamed,' he does not scold them but gently bids them, 'Tarry at Jericho until your beards be grown, and then return.' The chapter is teaching us a beautiful lesson in compassion — David shielded his servants from public shame and gave them time to recover their dignity. So our King deals tenderly with His servants when they are humiliated in His service; He does not despise the shamed but covers and restores them. We are taught both to refuse the suspicious spirit that wrongs the kind, and to imitate David's gentle care for those who suffer reproach for our sake.

And when the children of Ammon saw that they had made themselves odious to David, Hanun and the children of Ammon sent a thousand talents of silver to hire them chariots and horsemen out of Mesopotamia, and out of Syriamaachah, and out of Zobah. So they hired thirty and two thousand chariots, and the king of Maachah and his people; who came and pitched before Medeba. And the children of Ammon gathered themselves together from their cities, and came to battle. And when David heard of it, he sent Joab, and all the host of the mighty men. And the children of Ammon came out, and put the battle in array before the gate of the city: and the kings that were come were by themselves in the field. Guidance: Having made themselves 'odious to David,' the Ammonites compound their folly by pouring out a thousand talents of silver to hire a vast force of Syrian chariots and mercenaries — thirty-two thousand chariots and the armies of several kings. They spare no expense to wage a war they themselves provoked by their insolence. The chapter is showing us the costly, escalating nature of sin and pride: rather than humble themselves and seek peace, the Ammonites spend a fortune to multiply their enemies and entrench their rebellion. The chapter is teaching us how one act of folly, unrepented, leads to greater folly; Hanun's insult demanded a war, and the war demanded a ruinous outlay of silver and the gathering of mighty hosts. Sin is a hard master that keeps raising the stakes. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the wicked often go to enormous trouble and expense in their opposition to God's people — hiring help, massing armies, sparing nothing — and yet, as we shall see, all their hired strength avails nothing against the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that no amount of silver, no multitude of chariots, can secure a cause that stands against God and His anointed. When David hears of the great host, he simply sends Joab and the mighty men — confident, for he knows that the battle is the LORD's. The chapter is teaching us not to be dismayed by the costly and impressive preparations of those who oppose the right; the thirty-two thousand chariots of Ammon's hire would soon flee before the people of God. The pride that will not repent only piles up its own destruction.

The pride that will not repent only piles up its own destruction.

Now when Joab saw that the battle was set against him before and behind, he chose out of all the choice of Israel, and put them in array against the Syrians. And the rest of the people he delivered unto the hand of Abishai his brother, and they set themselves in array against the children of Ammon. And he said, If the Syrians be too strong for me, then thou shalt help me: but if the children of Ammon be too strong for thee, then I will help thee. Be of good courage, and let us behave ourselves valiantly for our people, and for the cities of our God: and let the LORD do that which is good in his sight. Guidance: Caught between two armies, 'before and behind,' Joab proves himself a wise and godly captain. He divides his forces, takes the harder front himself against the Syrians, gives the rest to his brother Abishai, and arranges for mutual support: 'If the Syrians be too strong for me, then thou shalt help me: but if the children of Ammon be too strong for thee, then I will help thee.' Then he speaks one of the noblest words in all the Old Testament: 'Be of good courage, and let us behave ourselves valiantly for our people, and for the cities of our God: and let the LORD do that which is good in his sight.' The chapter is showing us the perfect balance of faith and effort. Joab does everything wisdom requires — he plans, he positions, he arranges for help — and yet he leaves the outcome wholly in God's hands. The chapter is teaching us to combine our utmost diligence with complete submission to God's will: we are to 'behave ourselves valiantly,' to do our part with all our courage and skill, and then to say, 'let the LORD do that which is good in his sight.' This is the secret of the believer's labour — to work as if all depended on us, and to trust as knowing all depends on God. The chapter is teaching us the strength of brotherly support: 'if too strong for me, help me; if too strong for thee, I will help thee.' God's people are to stand together, ready to come to one another's aid in the day of battle. The chapter is teaching us the worthiest of motives: not personal glory, but 'for our people, and for the cities of our God.' Joab fought for the people and the honour of God, and that high cause nerved his courage. And it is teaching us holy resignation: whatever the result, 'let the LORD do that which is good in his sight' — content to leave the issue with God, sure that what He does is good. Here is how the Christian fights every battle: courageously, helpfully, for God's cause, and in glad surrender to God's will.

So Joab and the people that were with him drew nigh before the Syrians unto the battle; and they fled before him. And when the children of Ammon saw that the Syrians were fled, they likewise fled before Abishai his brother, and entered into the city. Then Joab came to Jerusalem. And when the Syrians saw that they were put to the worse before Israel, they sent messengers, and drew forth the Syrians that were beyond the river: and Shophach the captain of the host of Hadarezer went before them. And it was told David; and he gathered all Israel, and passed over Jordan, and came upon them, and set the battle in array against them. So when David had put the battle in array against the Syrians, they fought with him. But the Syrians fled before Israel; and David slew of the Syrians seven thousand men which fought in chariots, and forty thousand footmen, and killed Shophach the captain of the host. And when the servants of Hadarezer saw that they were put to the worse before Israel, they made peace with David, and became his servants: neither would the Syrians help the children of Ammon any more. Guidance: Joab's faith is vindicated: the moment he draws near, the mighty Syrian host 'fled before him,' and the Ammonites, seeing their hired help collapse, flee likewise. When the Syrians regroup and bring fresh forces from beyond the river, David himself takes the field and routs them utterly — slaying their captain and tens of thousands — until they sue for peace and will help Ammon no more. The chapter is showing us the LORD giving complete victory to His people: the hired thirty-two thousand chariots, the gathered kings, the fresh armies from beyond the Euphrates — all flee before Israel. The chapter is teaching us that when we do our duty courageously and leave the outcome to God (as Joab resolved in the previous verses), the LORD honours such faith with victory; Joab said 'let the LORD do that which is good,' and the LORD did good indeed, scattering the foe. The chapter is teaching us the emptiness of all human might arrayed against God's people; the silver was spent, the chariots were hired, the captains marshaled — and they 'fled before him.' No weapon formed against the LORD's anointed could prosper. The chapter is teaching us, too, the wisdom of pressing the advantage and finishing the work: David did not rest after the first rout but met the regathered Syrians and subdued them fully, so that they 'made peace... and became his servants.' The chapter is teaching us that the LORD turns the malice of His enemies to their own ruin and to His people's peace — the war Ammon's pride provoked ended in the submission of Syria and the safety of Israel. What the wicked devise for harm, God overrules for the good of His own and the honour of His name.
